transcript.text: How are you?

I'm usually talking to folks in the Bay.
I know.
It's great.
It's a little gloomy right now, but I love it.
I love not being in the Bay, to be honest.
How long have you been in Tennessee?
Oh, gosh.
Five years.
Before that, I was in New York for a long time, more than 10 years.
Super cool, yeah.
I'm in Houston, Texas myself, but also lived out in the Bay, in New York City.
Houston's home for me and my fiancé, so we decided to live close to the built-in babysitters, our parents.
Yeah.
Can't say no to that.
Cool.
Sorry for being a few minutes late, by the way.
The day just keeps going.
Yeah, no problem.
And just to introduce myself, my name's Tyler.
I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX, so.
So my plan is really just to learn more about, you know, your interest in our services and give you a good overview of how we partner, what we really specialize in.
And also, I saw your comment.
I'll tell you about how the AI workflows are created and are also involved in the content creation process.
So, yeah, I would love like a quick intro from you and just a bit more context on how you found us.
Yeah, of course.
I, yeah, I'm jenny.
lead marketing endeavor here at LiveKit.
Been here for four months, I think now.
And before that, I was at MongoDB for six and a half years.
My background is in product marketing.
So I have worked pretty closely with, let's say, content teams over the years.
We have a small team here at LiveKit today.
So that's something that I'm working on, sort of figuring out how we, how we scale and who we need to hire.
So today it's really just.
Me and one developer advocate, but we're looking to have a product marketing function.
That's not me as a part-time job.
I'm looking to have some more developer advocates join, help with our technical content.
We have growth marketing, field marketing, marketing ops as other roles that I'm looking at.
But I would say like on not on the team side, but on sort of just like, what are we trying to do?
Marketing at LiveKit.
I really want to just grow awareness.
think, you know, we play very much in like the voice and video AI space.
We have a platform that makes it really easy for people to build agents that use voice and video AI.
And so because the space is still relatively new, I think there's just a lot of opportunity for us to be seen as like a thought leader and the authority in the space.
And, you know, we have a lot of great, really smart people on the engineering side.
I don't think we've.

Well, we didn't have a marketing person before I joined, so there wasn't a ton going on, but I think we're really thinking about A, like really technical content, examples, demos, like how can we get that out through the DevRel team?
But then on the more thought leadership side, I think there's just, sorry, there's kind of three tracks.
There's like that leadership, there's educational content, which is a blend of my team and what we call our developer products team.
So they own documentation.
We've been partnering with them on some more of the like, not product specific, but kind of just like foundational content.
And then there's the technical content piece.
I haven't had a lot of time, to be honest, to like think through what we need to do.
I heard about you guys, because actually one of our board members sent this my way and said, hey, have you checked them out?
And so I said, no, you know what, worth a call.
Yeah.
Yeah, maybe I'll pause there.
Yeah, that's, that's super helpful.
Would you say like, is Vappy one of your main competitors or are they not a competitor?
Competitor at all.
I know the space sort of well, and that will help me answer, I guess, some of the questions.
Yeah, Spappy is one of the competitors.
I would say the others that we kind of pay attention to, Daily or Petcat.
Retail is interesting because they're a competitor, but also technically a customer of LiveKit.
Same with Eleven Labs.
And then like a Decagon or Sierra are also people kind of in the sphere.
Interesting.
Customers and competitors.
How does that one work?
Yeah, well, because LiveKit has, we offer like a networking infrastructure layer.

And so those customers that I mentioned and others, especially our kind of more legacy customers who use us for like live streaming and video conferencing, they use us for kind of kind that networking piece and not for like the agent framework or.
Or.
The agents platform that we're building.

So they, yeah, they make it, we make it easy for them to like actually transport the voice and video data to their users, from their servers to their users, but they're not kind of building agents with the SDKs that we offer.
That makes sense.
And then the like most common customer is building voice agents and even like video AI.
That's our ICP.
Yeah.
We're looking for companies who are actually building their own.
Yeah.
So some customers, examples, we have like Sort Health is a big customer.
They offer a platform for building, for like healthcare clinics to do like front office scheduling and prescription reminders and stuff with these like voice agents.
And so anytime someone calls, you know, they're directed to an agent and not like a human at the, at the healthcare clinic or like purple fish uses us for like AI recruiting.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Or they offer AI recruiters.
like, there's some people who, like, take us, kind of package them up into, like, a vertical-specific kind of solution.
So those are two examples.
Or we have people who are directly building for their kind of in-house call centers or whatever that they need voice agents for.
Okay, cool.
Yeah.
One of the reasons I ask is we made these, like, voice agent pages for Vappy.
And it was, like, in the early growthx days before we, like, created.
So they're not, like, an active partner with us right now.
We're not creating content for them.
But they were, like, a very early partner.
So, like, we built a formal partnership program that didn't exist when we had done that use case.
It's probably better for our conversation, right?
Like, that we're not actively partnered with one of your top competitors.
And I think it's a good thing, too, in the sense that, like, we know the space and have created content that could be, like, similar to content that you guys would want.
To create at LiveKit.
Let me go through like a quick presentation just to give you better context.

And then I'll show you some examples so that we can hone in on like what use case could make sense to consider when, you know, when you're ready to start really like thinking about a partner for content and the use cases that you want to execute on.
Sounds good.
Cool.
All right.

So like one, I think of the big reasons we're experiencing the most demand I've ever seen in my career at GrowthX is the shift in content marketing is something like every CMO is talking about right now.
You know, how can I show up in AI search?
People are having their first sales conversation with ChatGPT.
If we're not in the answer, it's like we don't exist.
And you see this from like clicks disappearing to the website.
But what's nice if you can start showing up in AI search is that AI search visits are 4.4 times more likely to convert.
They spend three times as long.
And it makes sense, right?
Like they're familiar with your company by the time they hit your website.
And so what we're helping with is both sides of search, right?
We're not like neglecting SEO.
Our approach is definitely looking at keyword analysis, search volume, ranking difficulty against competitors.
But we can create content that allows you to scale with quality.
So we do daily publishing for all of our customers.
And the idea is that if you're going to show up in AI search, part of the reason why is because you have the surface area of content that's required, right?
You just need a lot more content for the ways that people can search today in such a personal way.
And so our AI workflows, they go through like the most important factors for GEO.
Is the content well-researched?
We've got a deep researcher agent.
Is it accurate?
AI is known to hallucinate.
We use a fact checker agent, and I'll show you a demo.
Can you attribute it to credible sources?
Is the content fresh?
Thinking about if you haven't updated a blog article in over a year, that's an opportunity to squeeze some more juice out of the content.
And so we're really fitting in in today's world where teams are much smaller, right?
There's nobody out there hiring a massive team of editors and content marketers and graphic designers.
You could buy tools to build workflows and create content with AI, but will that become cost prohibitive when you're using tokens, so many tokens, API calls?
And in the traditional SEO agency, we're typically replacing them because we're involved from strategy all the way to staging and publishing and even delivering LLM visibility and analytics.
So think of us as like an end-to-end partner for driving organic traffic from the audience that you care about, and our focus is really more content for SEO and GE.
So a lot of the times it is editorial in nature.
Any questions that you have at a high level?
This is just trying to give you like the most relevant context up front so that you know what we do and how we typically work.
Nope, super, super good.
How relevant do you feel like it is to where your focus is at right now?
Like just too early to tell?
Or would you say that like editorial content is something that you're looking to get some, you know, some help with content creation around?
Yeah, definitely looking for, I would say not like actively looking for help in the sense that I'm not like opening rules specific to this or looking at other agencies right now.
But we've been talking about internally how we, yeah, scale content, but more so the other two pieces that I mentioned earlier.
So like, you know, hiring more developer advocates to like build the demos and like record videos on that.
Educational content.
We're just starting to like do a project to kind of create almost like an online course, but resources are very limited.
And then I think on the thought leadership side, the challenge is that we are still a pretty small company.
So the founders are very involved.
And so, you know, for example, when we have like a product launch, you know, they care a lot and spend a lot of time on sort of the positioning of how we want to do that stuff.

But I do think there's still an opportunity for just, you know, more content where we can maybe extract some of the, you know, the key points that they think we should be covering and then have someone or have, you know, tools that help us shape that into something that's actually, you know, optimized for these tools.
Like you're saying, optimized for SEO, optimized for geo.
And then I think we've just been so reactive right now as well.
It's just like, okay, well, we know we have this launch coming up.
So we've got to work on that.
We know we've got, you know, this happening or.

This happening in the market, so I would love us to get to a place where we have line of sight into, oh, here's like our content roadmap for the next, you know, three months of thinking more proactively.
What are the gaps?
What are the opportunities for us to rank well for topics?
And I feel like, again, I haven't done extensive research on this, but my hunch is that, you know, the search volume for a lot of these is low.
So, but for the long tail, I'm sorry, I'm still so used to SEO language from my experience, I'm just going to use that.
like, you know, there are going be like long tail keywords, it's all the same.

Yeah, like we kind of have an intuition about like what things are going to be more important or what things we think are, you know, maybe niche today, but like we really want to be known for, you know, being the best solution or being the thought leader or being the authority.
And so, again, just haven't really sat down and like, I wouldn't say I'm prepared to come to this call with like, hey, here are like the five topics that I know we need to know, but that is.
That's that I would love to, you know, either work with you guys on or figure out another way where we can start doing that.
I think in my mind, it was also like hire out the team a little bit and then I can have some more people on the team contributing as well.
Like I said today, it's kind of just me and one person cranking out as much as we can do.
It makes a lot of sense and like you could think of us as, you know, maybe like eliminating the need to hire, right, like a couple additional roles.
Like we could basically handle editorial for your team and then you could have like your developer advocates focus on more of the very technical content.
Not to say we can't do technical content, but what we could do is maybe just like open up more bandwidth with a small team to be able to execute at scale.
So just to continue kind of telling more about our story and get into some examples here really soon, do you know much about our founder and CEO, Marcel, and his background?
I think it would be helpful to like give you 30 seconds.
If not, I briefly looked him up when I looked on your webpage, but go for it.
Yeah, for sure.
Basically, the short story is that he's always believed brands should be more like publishers at IBM.
He built like this website called securityintelligence.com that recently got folded back into their parent site, but it was their highest ROI marketing channel for like several years.
And every stop in his career, he's done something similar.
So he was like the marketing leader at HashiCorp from six to 100 million, the CMO at scale through the acquisition by Meta.
And then at Deepgram, instead of building out this team, right, because it takes a lot of capital, takes a lot of time, he built AI workflows to replicate a lot of what he calls the messy middle.

So brief generation, deep research, drafting an outline, doing the fact-checking, and the results were so powerful that like he started doing these workshops and the demand was enough that he started GrowthX because of it.
So like
Part of what we're doing here is we've got our own platform that powers the service that we're providing to our clients, but we're actually like bringing clients into that platform.
We're a young company, right?
But like we're growing really fast.
And so you've got part platform, part experts in the loop.
So every client gets a managing director, managing editor, content manager.
We've got like a killer team.
know, our chief content officer was the editor-in-chief at TechCrunch for over a decade.
We've got folks who've like built out the G2.com website or ran SEO for TripAdvisor and Fanatics.

But there's also this element of we're building our own platform.
Clients will have access to that.
And so kind of just helps, helps put some spotlight on like how we're different than.
Traditional agency partner, hopefully.

We created to basically the blog they were working on internally.

built...

I'm more curious about how, how we get to this point.
Like, what, what does that look like?
Yeah, for sure.
It's right, the number one question.
So the way that we work, we do an eight week strategy sprint, and then a 12 month growth execution.
In the strategy sprint, our focus is developing these really rich artifacts that give the context necessary for the workflows to be effective and relevant.
So, so.
So.

So, you know, think about like audience personas as one of the artifacts, company context where we're going super deep on your business, writing guidelines so that we can calibrate the voice, and we'll even start publishing in phase one so that you know what you're getting out of the daily execution in phase two.
And so to show you an example here, here's one of our customers' workspaces, engine, they're in the business travel space.
We created these artifacts for them, go back, across all of their target industry verticals.
So we've got, you know, different artifacts for their personas.
And then if you look at the construction artifact, it's like everything about this ICP, right?
Like who are they, what are their fears, motivations, how do you win or lose their trust, what gets them fired, what gets them promoted?
And then once we've got these artifacts ready to go, that's when we can use workflows to scale quality.
And so this is a different.
Workspace, Biologica, they're in the healthcare space.
And so just an example of like how technical we can get again here, this is almost like medical advice basically around their supplements.
What we're starting with is we're starting with the keyword, a direction, and then the artifacts.
And then that's allowing us to, in this first step, create a brief.
And the second step, do deep research against the brief.
And then so on and so forth until we have final article with cover images ready to go.
And so what's cool about our platform here is that all of our workflows are built in code.
And so we can make changes really quickly.
We can create custom pipelines.

And if I inspect any of these steps, you'll see that for the brief step, we're fetching keyword traffic, organic results, search ranking factors, competitor content, and all of these steps and all these different APIs we would use to run this research or look at these analytics around search.

This is informing the output, and so this informed the brief, but then in the research step, we generate these research questions, perform deep research, and then validate with credible sources to, you know, continue to inform how the content gets put together.

All the while, our team is able to be in the loop, so that, you know, with the context that we have, all those different artifacts, they can make changes to the content in real time, or even, right, instruct the AI, you know, turn this into bullets, right?
And so this is just like a little bit of how the content gets created in the background, and all the while, we're evolving these workflows, and so, you know, our research is now agentic.
In this example for Augment, what we're doing is we're generating a research plan, and then we are making sure that when we look at the results,
So it meets a certain quality that we define with the client.
And if it doesn't meet that quality, it's going to rerun the research, right, until it gets there.
So, yeah, that's just like a little bit of the product behind the scenes.
Any questions about how the content, how the sausage is made, as they say?

Yeah, just not like anything extensive.

And we do like actively review the content with you.
You know, we create a roadmap of assignments that are going to be connected to different topic clusters where you want to be the authority, right?
And where we see some opportunities with search as well.
So we just take like a very audience first approach and we're measuring daily with our clients.
And really just like making sure that before we start, we understand like, you know, what's what's the KPI that you're ultimately trying?
We're to move by building more of an organic content engine by getting more AI search visibility.
Got it.

Actually, on that last thing you just flashed up, do you also provide reporting so that we wouldn't need something like Ahrefs and Profound, or are those still tools that we would use alongside working with you?
Yeah, absolutely.

Our CEO, Marcel, he always says that on calls, that if you work with GrowthX, you don't need an SEO agency, and you don't need a tool like Profound, because it's all in one, but we also do all the creation and execution, which you would have to do on your own using those different tools.
Okay, so you would be able to provide similar visibility into how our brand is being surfaced through the different LLMs today?
100%, in like a custom way, you know, like there's prompts that you care about, there's topics that you care about.
Connected to all the different LLMs, like ChatGPT, Gemini with Google, Perplexity, Deep Research.
Yeah, so I think, you know, we typically have like a four to six week queue to our strategy sprint kickoffs.
We like limit to two a week in terms of like how many clients we're working with, since there is like a lot of human involvement.
So just kind of keep us in the loop when you get a bit further, you know, into the journey of deciding which direction you want to go.
And I'm happy to set up another call, you know, anytime it would be helpful.
Okay, yeah, we'll do that.
One last question that just came to mind.
When we get to, let's say we go through with the strategy sprint, we're in the implementation or like the content growth execution phase.
How much time do you find on average, like the clients will spend with you to either like review or kind of meet on a, I don't know, weekly or monthly basis?
Yeah, it's a good question.
We typically like to do weekly meetings.
For as long as it takes to get to a place where there's not as much like review needed with the content.
But if it's a bandwidth thing with the team, it can all be done async.
We'll have a shared Slack channel.
We'll put, you know, the drafts in a Google Docs so that the team can quickly give feedback or suggest changes.
So, yeah, I mean, it definitely is hands-off and growth execution if we're in a place where we can review content efficiently async.
Okay.
Oh, sorry.

We're over time.
But this is helpful.
